prompt: What are the units of momentum?
explanation: Momentum p = mv has units kg·m/s (equivalently N·s).

prompt: Which is the formula for momentum?
explanation: Momentum is mass times velocity, p = mv.

A 0.43 kg ball is brought from 20 m/s to rest in 0.1 s. What average force acted on it?
explanation: Δp = mv = 8.6 kg·m/s, so F = Δp/Δt = 8.6/0.1 = 86 N.

prompt: A 2 kg ball is brought from 6 m/s to rest in 0.2 s. What average force acted on it?
explanation: Δp = mv = 12 kg·m/s, so F = Δp/Δt = 12/0.2 = 60 N.

prompt: A 1 kg ball is brought from 10 m/s to rest in 0.5 s. What average force acted on it?
explanation: Δp = mv = 10 kg·m/s, so F = Δp/Δt = 10/0.5 = 20 N.

prompt: A 0.43 kg ball is brought from 30 m/s to rest in 0.1 s. What average force acted on it?
A 3 kg ball is brought from 8 m/s to rest in 0.4 s. What average force acted on it?
explanation: Δp = mv = 24 kg·m/s, so F = Δp/Δt = 24/0.4 = 60 N.

prompt: A 5 kg ball is brought from 4 m/s to rest in 0.2 s. What average force acted on it?
explanation: Δp = mv = 20 kg·m/s, so F = Δp/Δt = 20/0.2 = 100 N.

prompt: Using the area under the force-time graph, what impulse does a constant 200 N delivered over 0.1 s provide?
explanation: Area of the rectangle = F × Δt = 200×0.1 = 20 N·s.

prompt: Using the area under the force-time graph, what impulse does a constant 150 N delivered over 0.2 s provide?
explanation: Area of the rectangle = F × Δt = 150×0.2 = 30 N·s.

prompt: Using the area under the force-time graph, what impulse does a constant 300 N delivered over 0.1 s provide?
explanation: Area of the rectangle = F × Δt = 300×0.1 = 30 N·s.

prompt: Using the area under the force-time graph, what impulse does a constant 80 N delivered over 0.5 s provide?
explanation: Area of the rectangle = F × Δt = 80×0.5 = 40 N·s.

prompt: Using the area under the force-time graph, what impulse does a constant 120 N delivered over 0.25 s provide?
explanation: Area of the rectangle = F × Δt = 120×0.25 = 30 N·s.

prompt: Using the area under the force-time graph, what impulse does a constant 250 N delivered over 0.1 s provide?
explanation: Area of the rectangle = F × Δt = 250×0.1 = 25 N·s.

Two players collide in mid-air with no external horizontal forces. The total momentum of the pair afterward is:

choices.label: The same as before the collision

explanation: With no external force, total momentum is conserved — unchanged by the collision.

prompt: The impulse-momentum theorem says the impulse on an object equals its:
explanation: J = FΔt = Δp — impulse equals the change in momentum.

prompt: Why does following through on a kick (extending contact time) help?

choices.label: It lowers the force to zero
Longer contact time delivers more impulse, so more momentum

explanation: J = FΔt: keeping force on the ball for longer Δt delivers more impulse and final momentum.

prompt: A keeper "gives" with the ball, catching it over a longer time. For the same change in momentum, this:

choices.label: Reduces the force on the hands

explanation: Δp is fixed; F = Δp/Δt, so a larger Δt means a smaller force.

prompt: Two identical balls have the same speed but move in opposite directions. Their momenta are:

choices.label: Equal in magnitude, opposite in direction

explanation: Momentum is a vector; same mass and speed but opposite directions give opposite-sign momenta.

prompt: Which is equivalent to one newton-second (N·s)?
explanation: N·s = (kg·m/s²)·s = kg·m/s, the units of momentum.

prompt: During a collision between two balls, the impulse each exerts on the other is:

choices.label: Equal in magnitude and opposite in direction

explanation: By Newton’s third law the forces (and so impulses) are equal and opposite.

prompt: In a perfectly inelastic collision, which quantity is definitely conserved?
explanation: Momentum is conserved in all collisions; kinetic energy is lost in inelastic ones.
